coredata.dc:title: Simultaneously fabrication of free and solidified N, S-doped graphene quantum dots via a facile solvent-free synthesis route for fluorescent detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Chang
Hai, Xin
Chen, Xu-Wei
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: A facile one-step solvent-free synthesis approach is proposed for the simultaneous fabrication of free and solidified N, S-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) by using citric acid as precursor and L-cysteine as dopant. Graphene nucleus is firstly formed via the intermolecular dehydration of citric acid. N and S are then incorporated into the graphene structure by attacking the margin of graphene nucleus. The cross-linking among the graphene nucleus via the intermolecular condensation leads to the generation of free N, S-GQDs, while the intermolecular amidation between L-cysteine molecules and graphene nucleuses contributes to the solid-state fluorescence graphene quantum dots (SSF-GQDs). The free N, S-GQDs exhibit favorable photoluminescence behaviors such as high fluorescent quantum yield of 74.5%, stable photoluminescence within a wide range of pH and high tolerance to external ionic strength of up to 1.0molL−1 NaCl, making it excellent fluorescence probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0.01–3μM and a detection limit of 3.3nM. The solidification of GQDs prevents the aggregation of GQDs efficiently and offers the solidified N, S-GQDs yellow-green fluorescence, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 10.6%. This proposed protocol provides a novel avenue to fabricate diverse fluorescent graphene materials for different practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solvent-free synthesis
N
S-doping
Graphene quantum dots
L-cysteine
Citric acid

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs
Fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ with free N, S-GQDs
Fabrication of free N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs
Characteristics of N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs
Optical properties of free N, S-GQDs
Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ with free N, S-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of N, S-GQDs (A). High-resolution TEM image of individual N, S-GQDs (B). AFM image of N, S-GQDs (C). TEM image of SSF-GQDs (D).

Full range XPS spectra of free N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs (A and B). High-resolution C1s spectra (C and D), high-resolution N1s spectra (E and F), high-resolution S2p spectra (G and H) of N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs.
XRD patterns of free N, S-GQDs (A) and SSF-GQDs (B). Raman spectrum of the obtained N, S-GQDs (C). FT-IR spectra of N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs (D).

UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence excitation/emission spectra of N, S-GQDs (A). The PL spectra for N, S-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths (B). Inset: Photographs of N, S-GQDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right, 365nm). The PL decay curves of N, S-GQDs (λex/em 377/420nm) (C). The PL behaviors of N, S-GQDs at different irradiation time (D), pH values (E) and ionic strength (F).

(A) The Photographs of SSF-GQDs under daylight (A) and UV light (B, 365nm). The 2D-fluorescence topographical map of SSF-GQDs (C). Fluorescence emission spectra of SSF-GQDs (excited at 340nm) and dissolved SSF-GQDs in NaOH (D). The Photographs of the fingerprints on filter paper and black paper using SSF-GQDs under daylight (E and G) and UV light (F and H, 365nm).

The normalized PL intensity of N, S-GQDs and undoped-GQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+(A). Fluorescence spectra of N, S-GQDs (0.1μgmL−1) with different concentration of Fe3+(from top to bottom: 0.02, 0.06, 0.80, 1.50, 2.50, and 3.0μM, respectively) (B). The linear curve of the (F0-F)/F0 vs Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 3.0μM (C). Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of the N, S-GQDs (0.1μgmL−1) in the presence and absence of Fe3+(3.0μM) and other different metal ions (60.0μM) (D).
Scheme for the preparation of free N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs with citric acid as precursor and L-cysteine as dopant.
Fluorescence quantum yields of some reported GQDs via bottom-up approaches.
Comparisons of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+detection.
Determination results of Fe3+ content in human blood samples by the proposed method (n=5).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Blood sample is diluted 100-fold for Fe3+ determination.

head.ce:title: Simultaneously fabrication of free and solidified N, S-doped graphene quantum dots via a facile solvent-free synthesis route for fluorescent detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile one-step solvent-free synthesis approach is proposed for the simultaneous fabrication of free and solidified N, S-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) by using citric acid as precursor and L-cysteine as dopant. Graphene nucleus is firstly formed via the intermolecular dehydration of citric acid. N and S are then incorporated into the graphene structure by attacking the margin of graphene nucleus. The cross-linking among the graphene nucleus via the intermolecular condensation leads to the generation of free N, S-GQDs, while the intermolecular amidation between L-cysteine molecules and graphene nucleuses contributes to the solid-state fluorescence graphene quantum dots (SSF-GQDs). The free N, S-GQDs exhibit favorable photoluminescence behaviors such as high fluorescent quantum yield of 74.5%, stable photoluminescence within a wide range of pH and high tolerance to external ionic strength of up to 1.0molL−1 NaCl, making it excellent fluorescence probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0.01–3μM and a detection limit of 3.3nM. The solidification of GQDs prevents the aggregation of GQDs efficiently and offers the solidified N, S-GQDs yellow-green fluorescence, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 10.6%. This proposed protocol provides a novel avenue to fabricate diverse fluorescent graphene materials for different practical applications.

The simultaneous fabrication of free N, S-doped GQDs and solidified N, S-doped GQDs are achieved via the intermolecular dehydration and intermolecular amidation; the obtained GQDS can be used as excellent fluorescence probes for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and fingermarks.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a one-atom-thick planar sheet of sp2-bonded carbon atoms considered as the first two-dimensional (2D) crystal [1,2], and has attracted enormous interest due to its extraordinary properties and potential applications [3–5]. In principle, the zero optical band gap of the pristine graphene prevents it from being optical luminescent, resulting in the limited applications in opto-electronic field [6]. The ways to open the band gap of graphene by downscaling their sizes into nanometer-level [7], destroying the integrality of π system by introducing defects or modifying with functional groups [8], have been widely adopted and demonstrate very powerful in tuning graphene into luminescent materials.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are few atoms-thick graphene with a size less than 100nm. GQDs have some excellent characteristics origin from the structure of graphene, such as high surface area, larger diameter, and better surface grafting using the π-π conjugated network or surface groups and other special physical properties [9]. Moreover, GQDs own excellent photoluminescence properties due to the strong quantum confinement and edge effects [10], which gains GQDs wide applications in biological imaging, fluorescence sensing and optoelectronic devices [11–16]. Generally, the preparations of GQDs fall into two catalogs, the strategies of “top-down” way and “bottom-up” way. The top-down strategy is based on cleaving or breaking down of carbonaceous materials including graphite [13], graphene [7], carbon fibers [17], carbon nanotubes [18,19], graphene oxide [20], and carbon black [21] via hydrothermal, strong acid oxidation and electrochemical exfoliation. In order to destroy the C–C bond of carbonaceous materials, long preparation time, harsh reaction conditions (strong acid or alkali) and complex separation procedures are usually needed in this strategy. In contrast to top-down approaches, the bottom-up strategy shows advantages of effective control on the morphology and size distribution of the products over mild synthetic conditions. Liu et al. report a bottom-up way to fabricate multicolor fluorescent GQDs with a uniform size of ~60nm and ordered morphology by using unsubstituted hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene as carbon source [22]. Meanwhile, the abundant organic precursors offer this strategy more selectivity to modulate the fluorescent properties for the obtained GQDs [23–26].

Doping GQDs with heteroatoms has been proved to be an effective way to modulate the band gap, tune electronic density and chemical activity of GQDs, which endows the heteroatoms-doped GQDs new optical phenomena and unexpected properties for practical applications [27,28]. The incorporation of N atom into the GQD structure not only facilitates the improvement of quantum yields [29], but also is useful to enhance the electro catalytic activity for oxygen reduction reactions [30,31]. Our previous work also demonstrates that the nitrogen-containing pyrrole-ring functionalized GQDs could lead to the red-shift of the emission maximum 550nm [32], which is highly desired in the field of bio-imaging. S atom is another atom widely adopted for GQDs doping. The doping with S can modify the electronic structure of GQDs effectively by introducing S-related energy levels between π and π* of C, leading to efficient and multiple emission peaks [33]. Li et al. indicate that the doped S atom in GQDs plays a crucial importance role on promoting the coordination interaction between Fe3+ and the phenolic hydroxyl groups of GQDs [34].

In most cases, the optimal properties of fluorescence GQDs are observed and investigated in aqueous phase under low concentrations, as aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) originated from π-π intermolecular interactions usually occurs for concentrated GQDs [35]. Some efforts have been taken to overcome the AIQ effect since fluorescent solid-state carbonaceous materials are quite popular in the field of forensic authentication, tracing analyses and diodes [36–38]. Zeng et al. prepare the intercrossed carbon nanorings (IC-CNRs) using PVA as source via a hydrothermal process. The high pure surface states of IC-CNRs formed by residual hydroxyl groups make the carbon ring support with each other, leading to effectively restrain their aggregation [39]. Chen et al. report a way to fabricate carbon nanomaterials with strong yellow-green solid-state fluorescence (SSF). The prominent steric hindrance from the surface PVA chains surrounding the graphitizing cores prevents the direct contact of cores with each other, which impress the self-quenching of graphitizing cores in the aggregation state [40].

In present work, the simultaneous fabrication of free N, S-doped GQDs and solidified N, S-doped GQDs are achieved via a solvent-free “bottom-up” approach by using citric acid as precursors and L-cysteine as dopant. Under oil-bath, graphene nucleus is firstly formed via the intermolecular dehydration of citric acid, and then N and S atoms of L-cysteine attack the margin of the graphene nucleus, leading to the incorporation of N, S into graphene structure. Afterwards, the cross-linking of formed graphene nucleus via the intermolecular condensation leads to the product of free N,S-GQDs, and the further cross-linking induced by forming acylamino oligomer among L-cysteine and graphene nucleus contribute to the product of solid-state fluorescence graphene quantum dots (SSF-GQDs). The doping of N, S in graphene structure brings the free N, S-GQDs favorable photoluminescence behaviors and high fluorescence quantum yield, making it excellent fluorescence probe for the sensitive detect of Fe3+ with a detection limit of 3.3nM. At the same time, the cross-linking among L-cysteine molecules and graphene nucleus prevent the aggregation of GQDs efficiently, making the SSF-GQDs as excellent fluorescent powder for the detection of fingermarks.

Citric acid (CA) is purchased from Damao Chemical Holiding Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). L-cysteine and FeCl3·6H2O are received from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HCl, NaOH and ethanol are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Metal chlorides or metal nitrates including HgCl2, CrCl3, Co(NO3)2, FeSO4, AgNO3, NiCl2, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2, BaCl2, CuCl2, MnCl2, Al(NO3)3, MgCl2, CaCl2, KCl and NaCl are the products of Bodi Chemical Holiding Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Human whole blood samples of healthy volunteer are provided by the Hospital of Northeastern University. All the chemical reagents are used as received without any further purification. Deionized water of 18MΩcm−1 is used throughout.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 2.100g of CA is uniformly mixed with 0.200g of L-cystenie, and then sealed in a 25mL bottom flask. The reaction mixture is heated and stirred with oil bath by refluxing at 200°C. The liquidation of the reaction mixture under high temperature turn it into a colorless solution firstly, then pale yellow, finally orange at 5min. After cooled down to room temperature, the orange product is dispensed into 10mL water. The supernatant collected after centrifugation is further purified through dialysis against pure water with cellulose ester membrane (MWCO: 100-500) for 48h, and then freeze-dried to obtain the free N, S-GQDs. The precipitate is collected by vacuum filtration and dried at 60°C to afford the final SSF-GQDs product.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images are recorded on a JEM-2100 (HR) field-emission electron microscope operating at 200kV (JEOL Ltd, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images are acquired on a Bruker Dimension icon atomic force microscopy (Bruker, Germany) using tapping mode with a tip type of Scanasyst air. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are conducted with an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo Instruments Inc, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns are recorded on a MPDDY2094 X-ray diffractometer (PAnalytical B.V, Netherlands) with Cu-Ka irradiation (l=1.5406Å) in the range of 2θ from 5 to 60°. Raman spectra are obtained using an XploRA Raman spectrometer (Horiba Co., France) with an excitation wavelength at 638nm. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) are recorded on Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA) from 400 to 4000cm−1. The fluorescence spectra (FL) are recorded on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) and equipped with a 1.0cm quartz cell. The excitation and emission slits are both set as 5.0nm, with a scan speed of 1200nmmin−1. UV–vis absorption spectra (UV–vis) are record on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). The quantum yield of the material is measured with a Quantaurus-QY absolute photoluminescence quantum yield measurement system (Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime is measured on an F980 photo counting system (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ with free N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The freeze-dried N, S-GQDs are firstly dissolved in de-ionized water with a concentration of 1.0μgmL−1. 20μL of N, S-GQDs solution, 10μL of Fe3+ solution (pH=3) and 170μL of de-ionized water are added into a 0.5-mL centrifuge tube and incubated at room temperature for 10min. Then the fluorescence intensity is recorded at 417nm by excitation at 340nm.

A calibration graph is derived on the basis of the relationship between the concentration of Fe3+and the fluorescence quenching values (F0-F)/F0 (the difference of the fluorescence intensity of N, S-GQDs in the presence/absence of Fe3+).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of free N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In present study, a bottom-up approach is adopted for the preparation of GQDs by using CA as the carbon source, as the carbonization of CA would produce graphite structures [24,41]. Scheme 1 illustrates the schematic scheme for the preparation of free N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs by using CA as precursor and L-cysteine as dopant. Under high temperature, the dehydration of citric acid inter-molecularly into sp2 domains leads to the generation of graphene nucleus with abundant carboxyl/hydroxyl groups. Then condensation reaction takes place between the carboxyl/hydroxyl groups of graphene nucleus and the amino group of L-cysteine [42,43], contributing to the incorporation of N, S atoms into the structure of graphene nucleus. The further nucleation among the graphene nucleus via intermolecular condensation leads to the formation of pyrrolic N in 5-member ring structures [44] and the 5-member thiazolidine/thiophene ring [45] in final product of N, S-doped GQDs.

Meanwhile, the intermolecular amidations taking place between the terminal carboxyl group of L-cysteine and graphene nucleus, and the intermolecular amidation among L-cysteine molecules, induce the formation of acylamino oligomer [46]. The consecutive multiple amidation reactions eventually result in a cross-linked polymer network with the generated GQDs as nodes. The cross-linking of N, S-doped GQDs via the amidation reactions among L-cysteine and GQDs restrain the aggregation of GQDs, offering the obtaining the solid-state GQDs with strong fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size distribution of the as-prepared free N, S-GQDs are shown in Fig. 1A. It can be seen that the obtained carbogenic nanoparticles possess quasi-circular shape, with uniform sizes ranging from 3 to 11nm (7nm average diameter in size). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1B) indicates that the obtained N, S-GQDs are highly crystalline with a lattice spacing of 0.240nm, corresponding to the (1120) crystal phase of graphene [17]. These results well demonstrate that the nanoparticles are composed of nanocrystalline cores of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms [47].

AFM analyses (Fig. 1C) illustrate that the obtained N, S-GQDs are of multi-layers with a topographic height near to 3.8nm, which is consistent with the layers of GQDs obtained by “bottom-up” method [24,48].

The obtained SSF-GQDs show a net structure with clear nodes (Fig. 1D). The crystalline part distributed in net nodes is the N, S-doped GQDs, and the chain of net structure should attribute to the condensed L-cystine via the intramolecular amidation reaction.

The XPS spectra of free N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs show four typical peaks of C1s, N1s, S2p and O1s ( Fig. 2A and B). The bulk element analytical results of XPS indicate that the free N,S-GQDs mainly contained C 65.84, O 28.80, N 2.66 and S 2.70wt% and SSF-GQDs are mainly composed of C 61.88, O 29.51, N 3.91 and S 4.70wt%, respectively. Indeed, the RN/C and RS/C of the SSF-GQDs are higher than the N,S-GQDs suggesting that SSF-GQDs have higher doping degree.

The high resolution spectra of C1s exhibit five main peaks (Fig. 2C and D), corresponding to sp2 C in C=C (graphitic structure) at 284.6eV, sp3 carbon in C-S, C-N and C-O at 285.4eV, 286.0eV and 286.4(286.6)eV, and C=O at 288.8(288.1)eV from carbonyls and carboxylates [47]. Comparing SSF-GQDs with free N,S-GQDs, the relative intensity of C=O increases because more acylamino bonds are formed between L-cysteine and graphene nucleus via the amidation reactions [46].

The N1s XPS spectrum of free N, S-GQDs displays a main peak at 400.2eV (Fig. 2E), which can be assigned to pyrrolic N (C-N), implying that N atom is bound to the C atom in the structure of GQDs with the form of five-member ring structure [32,49]. As for SSF-GQDs，the N 1s spectrum shows another peaks at 399.5eV and 400.9eV (Fig. 2F) from amide-N (O=C-NH) and graphitic N (N-C3), respectively. Amide-N further demonstrates more acylamino bonds formed into the SSF-GQDs structure. Graphitic N which arises from sp2 hybridized N are bonded with three sp2 hybridized C neighbors [32], implying that N atoms are partially bound to C atoms in the hexagonal network of graphene.

The S2p XPS spectra of free N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs can be deconvoluted into two distinct components at 163.4 and 164.5eV, which are close to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the C–S–C covalent bonds, owing to their spin-orbit couplings [33,34] (Fig. 2G and H). Thereby, it can be inferred that the sulfur is mainly doped at defects of graphene in the form of thiophene-like structure [50,51] or thiazolidine ring [43,52].

The XRD pattern of the free N, S-GQDs ( Fig. 3A) shows a wide (002) peak around 22° corresponded to an interlayer spacing of 0.40nm. The interlayer spacing value of the N, S-GQDs is greater than that of graphite (0.34nm). The difference may be attributed to the presence of functional groups at the edges of the N, S-GQDs [52]. It could be seen that the highest sharp diffraction peak around 23° appears in the XRD patterns (Fig. 3B) suggesting that the obtained SSF-GQD with graphene structure of high crystallinity.

Fig. 3C shows the Raman spectrum of N, S-GQDs. It exhibits the characteristic “disorder” D band at 1330cm−1, corresponding to the presence of sp3 defects, and crystalline G band at 1609cm−1, related to the sp2 C–C bond. The relatively intensity of D and G band expresses the atomic ratio of sp3/sp2, which represents a degree of disorder about the material. The ID/IG ratio of N, S-GQDs is 0.60, suggesting that N, S-GQDs have highly crystalline nature and integrated graphite structure [44,53].

Fig. 3D shows the FT-IR spectra of the obtained N, S-GQDs and SSF-GQDs. Both of them exhibit the band of aromatic C=C skeletal vibration located in the vicinity of 1630cm−1, indicating the presence of graphite structure. The bands at about 2550cm−1and 1400cm−1 are originated from the stretching vibrations of S-H and C-N, which further demonstrate the successful doping of S and N elements into the graphene structure [47,54]. The broad absorption band peaking at around 3430cm−1, which is ascribed to the stretching vibration of O-H, and the stretching vibrations of C=O and C-O-C at 1720cm−1 and 1210cm−1, indicate that there are abundant oxygen-contained groups included in the structure of the obtained GQDs. It should be noted that a unique band at 1520cm−1 appears in the spectra of SSF-GQDs, which should be assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric deformation of protonated imine (=NH+) [55]. Meanwhile, the other two bands at 3100cm−1 and 837cm−1 represent the stretching vibration and plane bending vibration of N-H, respectively. In addition, the bending vibration of C=O bond in the spectra of SSF-GQDs splits into two peaks at 1730cm−1 and 1680cm−1, corresponding to the different chemical environment in carboxyl groups and amide linkage [47]. These well suggest the existence of acylamino bonds in SSF-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of free N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A shows the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra for the as-prepared free N, S-GQDs. The UV–vis spectrum indicates two typical absorption peaks centered at 245nm and 340nm, which are related to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transition of the C=O band, respectively. The π and π* states that lies in the σ-σ* gap mostly determine the optoelectronic features of carbon-based materials [10].

The excitation maximums of N, S-GQDs are 245nm and 340nm, agreeing well with the UV–vis absorption bands. The N, S-GQDs exhibit an excitation-independent fluorescence behavior in an excitation range of 300–380nm (Fig. 4B) with a emission maximum of 417nm, which might be attributed to the well-distributed sizes of N, S-GQDs [44,47]. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of free N,S-GQDs with excitation at 340nm is determined to be 74.5%.

As listed in Table 1, the proposed solvent-free method in present study offers a high QY with respect to other doping protocols. Our experimental results also indicate that the absolute QY of the obtained GQDs increases with the amount of dopant L-cystenie, suggesting the great effect of the co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur into the GQD structure on improving fluorescence properties. The reason might lie in that S and N are excellent electron donors, which can participate in the π-conjugating of GQDs to facilitate the generation of radiative recombination, leading to the improvement of QY.

The fluorescence lifetime decay curve of N, S-GQDs (Fig. 4C) shows a multiple exponential function (τ1=7.70ns, 33.05%. τ2=13.78ns, 66.95%), suggesting that the PL of the obtained N, S-GQDs with excitation at 340nm might originated from two sources, i.e., the combination of graphene core and surface defects [32,56]. The PL of N, S-GQDs almost remains no intensity loss even after continuous irradiation under a Xe lamp for 2h (Fig. 4D). Meanwhile, the photoluminescence of N, S-GQDs remain stable within a board range of pH 5–13 (Fig. 4E). Moreover, virtually no variation is observed for the photoluminescence at a high ionic strength, i.e., the tolerance of NaCl concentration is as high as 1.0molL−1 (Fig. 4F). The above observations well suggest the potential applications of N, S-GQDs powerful fluorescent probe in handling with real sample of different sources under harsh conditions.

The obtained SSF-GQDs powders are yellowish ( Fig. 5A) and exhibit yellow-green fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light of 365nm (Fig. 5B). 2D-fluorescence topographical map suggests that there are three PL emission centers with the excitation/emission maximum at 210/490nm, 280/500 and 450/500nm for SSF-GQDs (Fig. 5C). The fluorescence absolute quantum yield of SSF-GQDs with excitation at 450nm is measured to be 10.6%.

Fig. 5D illustrates the fluorescence behaviors of SSF-GQDs dissolved in 1.0mM NaOH solution. As the SSF-GQDs is formed through the consecutive amidation reactions among graphene nucleus and L-cysteine molecules, the strong alkali circumstance induces the breakage of the formed acylamino bond, and leads to the dissociation of SSF-GQDs to free N, S-GQDs. As a result, the dissolution of SSF-GQDs in NaOH results in the blue-shift of emission maximum and the dissolved SSF-GQDs exhibit a same emission maximum as that of free N, S-GQDs.

The steady-state behavior and high QY of SSF-GQDs make them excellent candidate as fluorescent power applied in fingermarks. Fig. 5 E, F and G, H illustrate the fingerprint effect of SSF-GQDs on filter paper and black paper, irradiating with daylight and UV light of 365nm, respectively. It can seen that clear fluorescent fingermarks could be achieved by using SSF-GQDs as the fluorescent indicator, suggesting that the obtained SSF-GQDs should be potential in the field of forensic authentication, tracing analyses and diodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ with free N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The special coordinate interaction between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl groups has been widely adopted as the principled for Fe3+ determination [58,59], thus the performance of the obtained N, S-GQDs in the detection of Fe3+ is investigated. Compared with un-doped GQDs, stronger fluorescence quench of free N, S-GQDs in the presence of Fe3+ is observed under same conditions ( Fig. 6A), suggesting the contribution by the doped N and S to the sensitivity improvement in Fe3+ detection. The improvement could be ascribed to the fact that the doping of N and S into the GQDs structure induces the modulation of chemical and electronic characteristics of final GQDs, which promotes the coordination interaction between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl groups on the edge of N, S-GQDs.

As show in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-GQDs at 417nm gradually decreased with the Fe3+ concentrations. The (F0-F)/F0 displays a good linear correlation with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0.01–3μM (Fig. 6C). The limit of detection is derived to be 3.3nM, which is much lower than those obtained by other probes for Fe3+ detection ( Table 2).

The fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of N, S-GQDs are measured in the presence of various representative metal cations, including Hg2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+, to evaluate their potential interfering effects on the detection system. As shown in Fig. 6D, significant quenching effect of N, S-GQDs in the presence of 3μM Fe3+ is observed, while no obvious changes for F/F0 are observed for other metal cations with a concentration high up to 60μM, suggesting this proposed Fe3+ detection system with the obtained free N, S-GQDs as probes are of highly selectivity.

The practical applicability of the proposed protocol is demonstrated and validated by analyzing the Fe3+ content in human blood sample. To ascertain the accuracy of determination, the sample is spiked with Fe3+ standards of different concentration. As shown in Table 3, the recoveries of human blood samples are in the range of 90.0–104.0%, indicating the good practicability of the obtained N, S-GQDs in detecting Fe3+ in complex samples.

We have proposed a fast, facile and eco-friendly procedure for the simultaneous fabrication of free and solidified N, S-GQDs via one-step solvent-free synthesis approach. The incorporation of N and S atoms in the structure of GQDs induces the modulation of chemical and electronic characteristics of final GQDs, which offers the obtained free N, S-GQDs improved fluorescence properties. The specific coordinate interaction between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl groups make the obtained free N, S-GQDs excellent fluorescence probe for Fe3+ detection with favorable sensitivity and selectivity. The intermolecular amidation reactions make it possible to achieve the solidification of GQDs, meanwhile to remain the favorable fluorescence properties for the obtained solidified GQDs, which might gain popularity in the field of forensic authentication, tracing analyses and diodes. In general, the present work provides a novel and sample avenue for the production of diverse fluorescent graphene materials to tailor to different demands in practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the Science Foundation (21235001), Fundamental Research Funds, for the Central Universities (N150502001, N140505003, N141008001).
